title: Mini pavlovas
subtitle: Individual meringue nests with a soft centre, filled with cream and fresh fruit.
excerpt: Mini pavlovas are individual meringue nests, each about the size of a fist, with the same crisp shell and soft marshmallow centre as a full pavlova. They bake faster than a large pavlova and portion easily for a dinner party. Fill each one with whipped cream and whatever fruit is in season.
sourceNotes: Individual meringue nests follow the same method as a full pavlova, scaled to single portions. The form is well established in British and Australian home baking, particularly in catering contexts where individual portioning is practical. The method follows the same New Zealand pavlova tradition: cornflour and vinegar added for a soft centre, low and slow baking, cooling in the oven.

recipe.freezeNotes: Meringue does not freeze. Store unfilled shells in a dry place at room temperature for up to 24 hours.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the meringue shells the day before and keep in a dry place. Fill just before serving.

recipeTools.notes: Or a hand mixer. Both are suitable for meringue.
For neat nests. A large spoon and the back of it works just as well.

content.text: Mini pavlovas bake faster than a full pavlova and are practical for a dinner party: everyone gets their own, you can fill them to order, and there is no cutting or serving at the table. The meringue is the same mixture, piped or spooned into eight rounds on two trays.
Fill them with whipped cream and fresh fruit. Raspberries and strawberries in summer; passion fruit pulp any time of year if you can find them; sliced ripe mango in winter.

items.prepNote: from large eggs, at room temperature

content.text: Heat the oven to 130°C fan (150°C conventional). Line two baking trays with baking paper.

Whisk {{egg-whites}} in a clean, dry bowl to stiff peaks. Add {{caster-sugar}} a tablespoon at a time with the mixer running, waiting between each addition, until the meringue is thick, glossy, and smooth. Fold in {{cornflour}} and {{white-wine-vinegar}}.

Divide the meringue into 8 equal mounds on the lined trays, spacing them well apart. Use the back of a spoon to flatten each into a rough disc about 9 cm across, with the sides built up slightly to create a shallow indentation in the centre for the cream.
Bake for 45 to 50 minutes until the surface is set and very lightly coloured. Turn off the oven and leave the meringues with the door ajar for at least 1.5 hours.
To serve: whip {{double-cream}} to soft peaks. Spoon a generous pile onto each meringue. Top with {{strawberries}} and {{raspberries}}. Serve immediately.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Mini pavlovas are slightly more forgiving than one large pavlova.

items.cause: Batter not stiff enough, or sugar added too quickly.
items.fix: The meringue must hold a stiff peak before going into the oven. If it spreads, the sugar was likely added in too large amounts before the whites were stable enough.

items.symptom: Bottoms sticky and hard to remove from paper.
items.cause: Too much moisture in the oven, or removed before fully cooled.
items.fix: Leave on the paper until completely cool. A silicone baking mat may work better than paper in very humid conditions.
